=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

revision.text:
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunahikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

The shrine is now part of [[Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:区市町村指定天然記念物
revision.text:
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The shrine is now part of [[Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Buildings and structures in Kōfu, Yamanashi
revision.text:
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category History of Kōfu City
revision.text:
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />

revision.text:
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.

The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />

revision.text:
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[:en:Mount Kinpu|Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[:en:Mount Kinpu|Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.

The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.

The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>

The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[:en:Mount Kinpu|Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|qid=Q201932}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]].

The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[:en:Mount Kinpu|Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
[[Category:Merged Shikinaisha autogenerations]]{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[:en:Mount Kinpu|Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
