revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[:en:Mount Kinpu|Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu]][[ja:金櫻神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647310]] Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]].
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[:en:Mount Kinpu|Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]], believed to have been established on [[:en:Mount Kinpu|Mount Kinpu]] in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu]] period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] * [[Kushinadahime]]

In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the [[:en:Tamagawa|Tamagawa]] in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}.
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship}}, believed to have been established on {{ill|Mount Kinpu|en|Mount Kinpu|WD=Q6921689|lt=Mount Kinpu}} in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}} period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|WD=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra}}.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the {{ill|Tamagawa|en|Tamagawa|WD=Q1905392|lt=Tamagawa}} in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: Kanazakura-jinja, Kofu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}.
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship}}, believed to have been established on {{ill|Mount Kinpu|en|Mount Kinpu|WD=Q6921689|lt=Mount Kinpu}} in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}} period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|WD=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra}}.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the {{ill|Tamagawa|en|Tamagawa|WD=Q1905392|lt=Tamagawa}} in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship}}, believed to have been established on {{ill|Mount Kinpu|en|Mount Kinpu|WD=Q6921689|lt=Mount Kinpu}} in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}} period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|WD=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra}}.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the {{ill|Tamagawa|en|Tamagawa|WD=Q1905392|lt=Tamagawa}} in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.

The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932|1=Kōfu}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}}}
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}.
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}}, believed to have been established on {{ill|Mount Kinpu|en|Mount Kinpu|WD=Q6921689|lt=Mount Kinpu|1=Mount Kinpu}} in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|WD=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}}.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the {{ill|Tamagawa|en|Tamagawa|WD=Q1905392|lt=Tamagawa|1=Tamagawa}} in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|WD=Q201932|1=Kōfu}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}}}
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}.
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}}, believed to have been established on {{ill|Mount Kinpu|en|Mount Kinpu|WD=Q6921689|lt=Mount Kinpu|1=Mount Kinpu}} in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|WD=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}}.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the {{ill|Tamagawa|en|Tamagawa|WD=Q1905392|lt=Tamagawa|1=Tamagawa}} in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|qid=Q201932|1=Kōfu}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}}}
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|qid=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}.
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}}, believed to have been established on {{ill|Mount Kinpu|en|Mount Kinpu|qid=Q6921689|lt=Mount Kinpu|1=Mount Kinpu}} in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|qid=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}}.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the {{ill|Tamagawa|en|Tamagawa|qid=Q1905392|lt=Tamagawa|1=Tamagawa}} in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
{{translated page|ja|金櫻神社|version=103447770|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanazakura shrine №1.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kofu|qid=Q201932|1=Kōfu}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.769431|N|138.556056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}}}
'''Kanazakura Shrine''' (金櫻神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located at Mitake Town 2347, [[Kōfu City]], {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|qid=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}.
== Overview == The shrine is situated in the northern part of Kōfu City, in the mountainous region upstream of the Arakawa River. It is a center of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}}, believed to have been established on {{ill|Mount Kinpu|en|Mount Kinpu|qid=Q6921689|lt=Mount Kinpu|1=Mount Kinpu}} in 465 during the reign of Emperor Yūryaku. Known as Zao Gongen until the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} period, the shrine was renamed "Kanazakura Shrine" in 1868 according to a historical document.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The surrounding area included [[Onshi|pilgrims’ lodging]] and [[shukubō]] accommodations, forming the temple-town Mitake Village.
The sacred object of worship is a granite boulder known as the "Five-Jō Rock," measuring approximately 20 meters in height. The enshrined deities include: * [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] * [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]] * [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]] * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} * [[Kushinadahime]]
The shrine treasures spherical crystals known as "Water Jewels" (two) and "Fire Jewels" (three).<ref>Koshimoto Fumiko, "Jewels of the Land of Gemstones, Shōsenkyō," ''Tranveer,'' March 2017, pp. 16–17.</ref>
== History == === Founding === According to shrine records, Yamato Takeru visited the area during his eastward expedition in the 40th year of Emperor Keikō’s reign and ordered [[Kai Kokuzō|Shiomi Sukunē]], the local governor, to construct the shrine. Later, in the 10th year of Emperor Yūryaku’s reign, a new shrine was built on Mount Mitake following a divine command. In 674, Zao Gongen and Konsei Daimyōjin were enshrined at the main and middle shrines respectively, after being transferred from [[Mount Kimpusen]] in Yamato Province.
=== General History === During the ancient period, the shrine received reverence from the provincial governors of Kai Province. The shrine is said to have been rebuilt by Yoshimitsu Minamoto, founder of the [[Takeda clan]], and later became a place of worship for the clan. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, Hōjō Tokiyori dedicated a [[Daihannyakyō]], and Nichiren offered the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|qid=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}}.<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref> The spring water flowing from the top of the shrine’s sacred rock was believed to be the source of major rivers, including the Arakawa and Aikawa in Kōfu, the {{ill|Tamagawa|en|Tamagawa|qid=Q1905392|lt=Tamagawa|1=Tamagawa}} in Musashi Province, and the [[:en:Chikuma River|Chikuma River]] in Shinano Province. As a result, the shrine also became a center for agricultural deities and rain prayers.
The shrine is known for its wolf-related [[kenshū]] worship, which parallels beliefs associated with [[Mitsumine Shrine]] and [[Musashi-Mitake Shrine]]. Paintings and documents describe the legend of a white wolf guiding Yamato Takeru to the shrine.<ref>{{sfn|Uekusa|2008|pages=}}</ref> Even today, wolf talismans are distributed at the shrine.
== Grounds == The original middle shrine and eastern shrine pavilions, burned in 1955, were believed to date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. The reconstructed structures, painted red, were completed in 1959. The shrine also features notable artifacts, including masks, lacquered boxes, and drums, designated as cultural properties by Yamanashi Prefecture.<ref name="kenshitei">[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/kougeihin.html Yamanashi Prefecture Cultural Properties List]</ref>
The shrine grounds also feature a rare [[ukon cherry]] tree.
== Annual Festival == The annual festival takes place from April 21 to 22 and includes traditional [[Shinto kagura]] performances. The shrine is now part of [[:en:Chichibu Tama National Park|Chichibu Tama National Park]] and is a popular tourist destination, alongside [[Shōsenkyō Gorge]].<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== Designated Cultural Properties == ; Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties The following items were designated on August 7, 1967: * Eight Noh masks<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Sumiyoshi-style lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Family crest lacquered box<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with raft motif<ref name="kenshitei" /> * Drum body with weapon motif<ref name="kenshitei" />
; Kōfu City Designated Natural Monuments * Cedar Grove of Kanazakura Shrine - Designated on March 1, 1977<ref>{{Citation needed|date=2021st year 10月}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist|30em}}
== Bibliography == * Uekusa Manabu, "Wolf Worship in the Kai Region," ''Yamanashi Prefectural Museum Bulletin,'' vol. 2, 2008. * ''Cultural Properties Lost to War,'' Cultural Affairs Agency, 2003, pp. 460–461.
